replies.author: @aWalkThroughLife
replies.text: @Truthwillout2177 On whether or not comment sections like these are echo chambers or focus groups, I think they carry traits of both; it depends on how you are using them. If you are simply looking for people to reinforce your beliefs on such topics, then you simply go where they are bound to gather (however, I have seen videos the Drinker has done where people go back and forth on whether or not they agree with him so not quite an echo chamber). If you are looking to see what people's thoughts are on such topic matters, you will find people in the comment sections who can give fairly insightful and succinct summaries and opinions on it (but you may have to sort through a lot of mindless comments on "yeah, he's right" to find them).

replies.author: @NegotiatorGladiarius
replies.text:
Which really is the whole point. They make it sound like you have to be a hater or even a fascist to be a creator on YouTube, but in fact there is no such barrier to entry. Anyone who wants to put their opinions in a video, CAN. And in fact it's easier if you're not an actual racist or fascist, because the AI tends to be over-sensitive to that. The only real filter here is that the viewers are free to subscribe or not, and up- and down-vote as they wish.

And that is the real problem. There are plenty of pro-Disney channels, and even some outright shills. Just last week a video showed up in my recommendations, where a bunch of them were 'reacting' in some obviously fake way to some SW scene. And I don't mean fake because they liked it, but because no normal person reacts with the only range being from shrieking like a fangirl at a concert to getting tears in their eyes at every other line in a movie. But then those get like a thousand subscribers and influence almost nobody.

So yeah, as the Drinker was saying, it's one hell of a focus group. You have a sample of millions as to who agrees and disagrees.

replies.author: @NegotiatorGladiarius
replies.text:
@Truthwillout2177 Does it matter, though, if they're echo chambers or not? The thing that actually killed such shows is actually viewership data on Disney+, not whether the Drinker liked it or not. Disney is going to look at that data, and not go, "Maan, it was a success, but if the Drinker disliked it we'll just have to cancel it" :p

So what you have at the end of the day is that some echo chambers seem pretty good at predicting whether something will be a success, and some which are the exact opposite. If you can figure out which are which, you've got a decent idea of what people actually want to see. I.e., you have a meta focus group, so to speak.

replies.author: @a.nonimus6705
replies.text: ​@DocMicrowave Yeah, that's another problem with The Acolyte. It took Star Wars — a series defined by an existential conflict between absolute good and absolute evil — and attempted to make it morally amiguous. This is not what fans of Star Wars want. I, personally, am not opposed to morally ambiguous stories. In fact, I usually enjoy them. But in a series starkly defined by the duality of absolutes, it's such a huge misfire that even the demographic it was targeting didn't like it. Granted it was terribly written, to an unbelievable and egregious extent, I would argue that even if it had been very well written it would've still been a total misfire because it was the literal opposite of what Star Wars is known to be. A perfect example of this same type of mistake is "Coke 2.0". Nobody cared that Coke 2.0 tasted better than original Coke. They wanted original Coke, that's why they were buying original Coke. You can't just change the product from what the customer expects, and somehow think the customer will be happy about it, even *if* that change is an improvement (not that The Acolyte was an improvement, just saying that even if it was an improvement it still would've been hated, because people do not want improved Star Wars, they just want Star Wars).

replies.author: @DocMicrowave
replies.text:
@a.nonimus6705 Could not agree with you more.  It is not just Star Wars.  There has been a turn in movies and media in recent years to blur the lines between what is good and what is evil.
Now its oppressive and ridged verses misunderstood and disadvantaged.
Star Wars was never about that.  There were fairly clear lines drawn between good and absolute Evil.
And people loved it.
Sure Vader redeemed himself at the end on episode 6.  But he had chosen an evil path to begin with and suffered the consequences of his choices in the end.

Today, evil is just misunderstood.  Sometimes forced into situations supposedly by the good guys.  Who often are depicted as not to righteous and are the real bad guys.  Headland her self said she doesn't believe in the concept of good vs evil.  So we get the Acolyte.  

We have come a long way since the original trilogy.  And not in a positive way.

replies.author: @a.nonimus6705
replies.text:
​@DocMicrowave I think Vader redeeming himself at the end of ep6 is also an illustration of good vs evil. As Vader himself wasn't evil, per se, he was just manipulated by evil, and when he saw the good that he could've been in his son, it compelled him to be good. Even in that moment where things seem a little ambiguous, the theme of good v evil shines right through anyway. This theme is very popular for a reason, because people in general like to see the good guys come out on top, and that simply cannot happen without very clearly defined good and evil.
I think the social divide is where the problem comes from. The people in charge of these productions have lost touch with the people watching the productions, and I think a big part of that is they've lost their moral compass. Without getting political, as I'm nonpartisan, but in 2016 everything in Hollywood seems to have changed. The Hollywood elite clearly didn't like the new president, which I'm not going to make any comment about one way or another, so he became the new "evil". Everything he represented had to be portrayed as "evil". What they didn't seem to realize is that he represented half the country. So Hollywood went on this crusade to paint half of America as evil, and in doing so, lost sight of what actual evil is. Because, let's be real, half of America is not evil, regardless of who they vote for. So Hollywood just gaslit so hard that they ended up gaslighting themselves. This seems to have been the shift that broke Hollywood. I think it's a testament to why politics and art should never mix. We have other examples in society of why politics and virtually anything else shouldn't mix, but for art in particular, Hollywood is the perfect example of it

​@a.nonimus6705Well said!  I pretty much agree on all points.  Though I would push that shift in Hollywood back a bit to start in 2015.

Especially pushing it in action movies, scifi, fantasy, genres where it definetly did not belong.  It destroyed the purpose of going to see those movies in the first place.  Entertainment, escapism, fascination.  
Last think you want to see in those films was subtle (in more recent years not so subtle) hints of Orange man bad, minority oppression, pushing of various current day social issues ..... In A Sword And Sorcery film!  Scfi flick set on another planet with alien races, and of course the ubiquitous super hero films.  
Everything had to send a (the) message.
You have there you activist creatives fresh out of film school thinking there is a problem with every successful classic film or franchise.  Feeling the needed to "fix" it by "reimagining" it.  There are a few veteran creators that fell for it as well and just went along with where they thought the wind was blowing. 
This also extended into TV shows.
They tried creating new and fresh stuff packed with all of the present day social talking points.  But that stuff was mostly DOA.  No audience, no interest.  So leaned heavily of popular IPs.  Movies and TV franchises that already came with a built in fandom.  Of course they always saw problems with the originals and setup out to not only fix them, but to use them as platforms for activision.
Suddenly there are too many Caucasian leading men (who are good guys), not enough Mary sues, not enough representation (there are quotas to be met for every new project), certain races/genders/persuasions must always be shown a certain way.
Hollywood swung the pendulum so far left it broke.  They even started tripping over themselves with there extreme prejudice d writing and castings.
Not casting real little people in snow white because it would be offensive, thus denying a minority community much needed work and visibility, was a perfect example of political correctness taken to epic levels of lunacy.

Anyway, it seems like Hollywood is trying to fix that pendulum now.  Of course, fatigue over loosing billions of the last few years might have had a little something to do with that.
It still remains to be seen if that actually start swinging the pendulum back towards closer to center.

They just flick through TV/streaming until they find something that looks worth watching - and watch it.

Star Wars used to get the benefit of a doubt without even waiting to see if it was worth watching.

The sequels and the succession of badly written/produced/acted TV series killed that benefit of a doubt.

Now when they see the cack that is Disney Star Wars they look, see and flip over to something else.

It's not quite voting with your wallet, but it's close enough.

Headland probably mentioned Youtubers because they were the few people who actually bothered to watch it all and leave a review of ANY kind.

What was done when I was at school wont be done today. Someone younger wont get it. That and I am English in England. When I was in junior school we still wrote with a fountain pen and traditional ink cartridges. Blotting paper and were taught italic writing as that was the trend in the school I was in. Others elsewhere were doing cursive. I hated the italic nonsense. It was a very slow way to carve out letters on a page. When I progressed to comprehensive it was all abandoned and different again, simple biro pens and write as fast as possible to cram in facts. It was a struggle to adapt my writing to keep up. And all the different regions from the town I still live in, had different methods taught to the pupils at the junior level. Then in the final comprehensive it was one size fits all and standardised. I went on to do O and A level at English and my natural wacky persona, cant resist messing about. More and more, worse and worse are a balanced description with both descriptions framing each other. replies.author: @justforever96
replies.text:
It can't be "corruption" unless it's actually illegal to do it. It's not even unethical, other than by general standards, there isn't some Code of Conduct for producers that says it's wrong to cast close family or friends. It's a private business and they are free to hire whoever they want for whatever reason, they aren't stealing money from people who entrusted it to them or violating a legal standard for hiring. It's just nepotism, and that's not illegal. 
And I am sure that if you investigated into it you would find that there were other people who signed off on it, she didn't just declare "my wife is playing this part" and that was that. They don't have dictatorial control, although they have a lot of influence to get what they want. Because they were put in charge. On the other hand the studio or shareholders are free to stop her of they don't like what she's doing and put someone else in charge.

replies.author: @justforever96
replies.text: ​@patrickwilliamson29nepotism isn't automatically bad, not every person hired because they were related is just automatically a terrible employee. But it increases the odds. It depends on whether they were hired _even though_ they are related, or _because_ they are related. Personally I have no problem with nepotism if it's your own business, you have a right to try to set your kids up. If you dragging your kids into someone else's employ it better be because they are extremely qualified. And it's probably not the wisest move unless you just don't care if people talk crap and say they just got hired because they are related.
